text.title: QAA Antipatterns
text.summary: Critical QAA failures: hallucinated SLAs, qualitative measures, symmetric (H,H) trees, scenario inflation, no human checkpoint on prioritization.

antipattern.description: LLM or analyst invents "industry standard 99.99% availability for SaaS dashboards" without citing a verifiable source.
antipattern.reason: Unbounded SLAs drive over-engineering. A 99.99% uptime target requires multi-AZ active-active deployment with automated failover — justified for payment processing, not justified for an analytics dashboard used 9-5 business days.

rule.statement: Every numeric response measure must cite its source: competitor SLA, internal incident data, regulatory requirement, or vendor documentation. No invented numbers.
rule.rationale: Cited sources survive review and re-evaluation. Invented numbers propagate through architecture decisions and become requirements with no grounding in reality.

antipattern.description: Scenarios with measures like "acceptable response time", "good user experience", "minimal downtime".
antipattern.reason: Qualitative measures cannot be automated into CI gates, cannot be tested, and mean different things to different stakeholders. They produce the same architecture regardless of the scenario priority.

antipattern.description: Utility tree where every quality attribute and every scenario is rated (H,H) — "everything is critical".
antipattern.reason: When everything is equally critical, the tree provides no prioritization value. Teams still build the architecture they wanted to build anyway, now with a decorative document attached.

antipattern.description: LLM generates the utility tree priorities and they are adopted without stakeholder review.
antipattern.reason: Importance is a business judgment that requires knowledge of revenue impact, competitive landscape, regulatory obligations, and customer commitments. LLMs infer this from training data patterns, not from the actual business context.

rule.statement: Always require human sign-off on the utility tree prioritization before it drives architecture decisions. This is the mandatory human-in-the-loop checkpoint in the QAA workflow.
rule.rationale: Human judgment on priorities is irreducibly necessary. The LLM's role is to generate candidate scenarios and structures; the human's role is to validate that the priorities reflect actual business risk.
